ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists specifically for strawberry earl grey tea latte. Mapped to the closest parent class 'tea beverage' (FOODON_03301099). A more specific term for flavoured milk tea latte is not available in FoodOn as of 2024.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A0FJP (tea-based beverage) is the closest defensible match. No specific code exists for strawberry earl grey tea latte with milk in FoodEx2 as of 2024.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code identified for this specific flavoured tea latte beverage.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard cup (350 g) is the typical serving size for this beverage as listed on the GrabFood menu.

nutrient_source.title: USDA FoodData Central — Milk, whole, 3.25% milkfat; Tea, brewed, prepared with tap water; Strawberry syrup (SR Legacy composite estimate)
nutrient_source.organization: U.S. Department of Agriculture, Agricultural Research Service
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No exact entry for Strawberry Earl Grey Tea Latte exists in USDA FoodData Central or Singapore HPB Foodo. Nutrient values are estimated from a composite of USDA SR Legacy entries: brewed Earl Grey tea (FDC ID 171917), whole milk (FDC ID 171265), and strawberry syrup/flavouring. The input values (energy 80 kcal, protein 2.29 g, fat 2.86 g, carbohydrate 10.86 g, sugar 10.0 g, sodium 34.29 mg, dietary fibre 0.0 g, calcium 80 mg, cholesterol 10 mg, saturated fat 1.71 g, trans fat 0.09 g, vitamin C 1.43 mg per 100 g) are consistent with a milk-tea latte diluted with brewed tea and sweetened with strawberry syrup, and are retained as the best available estimate. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, potassium_mg, iron_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, and remaining micronutrients set to -1 as detailed values are not available for this composite preparation in any approved source.

gi_source.title: Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC. International Tables of Glycemic Index and Glycemic Load Values: 2008. Diabetes Care. 2008;31(12):2281-2283. — Analogous estimate: sweetened milk-based tea beverage
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care (peer-reviewed journal)
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Strawberry Earl Grey Tea Latte. GI estimated at 55 based on the closest analogous food: sweetened milk tea / chai latte with added sugar, referenced from the University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. 2008 international GI tables. GI 55 falls at the upper boundary of the low GI range (≤55) per the University of Sydney classification system, and is therefore categorised as 'low'. The dominant glycaemic driver is the added sugar (10 g/100 g, predominantly sucrose from strawberry syrup). Glycemic load per serving calculated as: GL = (GI × carbohydrate_g_per_serving) / 100 = (55 × 38.0) / 100 = 20.9 ≈ 21.0, where carbohydrate per 350 g serving = 10.86 × 3.5 = 38.0 g.

hypertension.key_factor: High added sugar content (10.0 g/100 g) from strawberry syrup; sodium is low at 34.3 mg/100 g but added sugar independently elevates blood pressure via insulin-mediated sodium retention and sympathetic nervous system activation.
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should request an unsweetened version of this beverage without strawberry syrup or added sugar, as the 10 g/100 g sugar load (35 g per standard 350 g cup) exceeds the AHA recommended daily added-sugar limit and independently raises cardiovascular and blood pressure risk.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Sugar-sweetened beverage with 10.0 g sugar/100 g (35 g per serving) and zero dietary fibre; liquid sugar is rapidly absorbed, causing acute postprandial blood glucose elevation.
type2_diabetes.guidance: People with Type 2 Diabetes should avoid this beverage in its standard sweetened form; if consumed, request plain Earl Grey tea with unsweetened Hokkaido milk and no strawberry syrup, and monitor blood glucose response given the high glycaemic load of 21 per serving.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat (1.71 g/100 g, ~6 g per serving) from Hokkaido whole milk combined with added sugar (10 g/100 g) and trans fat (0.09 g/100 g) contribute to atherogenic risk; however, Earl Grey tea polyphenols (bergapten, catechins) may offer modest cardioprotective benefit.
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should limit consumption of this sweetened milk tea latte due to its saturated fat and added sugar content; choosing an unsweetened, reduced-fat milk version would substantially lower the cardiovascular risk profile.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Added sugar (10.0 g/100 g) from strawberry syrup promotes pro-inflammatory cytokine production, negating the anti-inflammatory potential of Earl Grey tea bergapten polyphenols.
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Individuals with rheumatoid arthritis should request an unsweetened version; plain Earl Grey tea with unsweetened milk would retain the anti-inflammatory polyphenol benefit without the pro-inflammatory sugar load.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Strawberry Earl Grey Tea Latte is a Japanese-café-inspired flavoured milk tea beverage sold in Singapore, consisting of brewed Earl Grey tea (black tea scented with bergamot oil) combined with creamy Hokkaido whole milk and sweetened with strawberry syrup. The drink is typically served chilled or at room temperature in a 350 ml cup. Its flavour profile is floral and citrusy from the bergamot, sweet and fruity from the strawberry syrup, and creamy from the Hokkaido milk. Per 100 g, it provides approximately 80 kcal, 2.29 g protein, 2.86 g fat (1.71 g saturated), 10.86 g carbohydrate, and 10.0 g sugar, with 34.3 mg sodium and 80 mg calcium. The high sugar content (10 g/100 g, 35 g per standard 350 g serving) classifies this as a sugar-sweetened beverage (SSB), making it unsuitable for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes, hypertension, or cardiovascular disease in its standard form. The glycaemic index is estimated at 55 (low, upper boundary) with a glycaemic load of approximately 21 per serving. Earl Grey tea contributes bergapten and catechin polyphenols with modest anti-inflammatory and antioxidant properties, but these benefits are substantially offset by the added sugar. The beverage is vegetarian and gluten-free but contains dairy (milk allergen). It is commonly found on café and bubble tea menus across Singapore.

confidence_reason: No exact entry for Strawberry Earl Grey Tea Latte exists in any approved primary nutrient database (HPB Singapore Foodo, USDA FoodData Central, ICMR-NIN, IMR Malaysia MyFCD). Nutrient values are estimated from a USDA SR Legacy composite of brewed Earl Grey tea, whole milk, and strawberry syrup, consistent with the described recipe. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database for sweetened milk-based tea. Confidence is therefore medium rather than high.
notes: 1. dietary_fibre_g is 0.0: confirmed as naturally zero for this brewed tea and milk beverage with no whole fruit or fibre-containing ingredients; consistent with USDA FoodData Central data for brewed tea and whole milk. 2. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, potassium_mg, iron_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid profile and full micronutrient panel for this composite tea latte preparation are not available in USDA FoodData Central or Singapore HPB Foodo as of 2024. 3. The input source URL (https://foodo.sg/) was listed as HPB Singapore but the record provenance is ai_generated with confidence 0.7; nutrient values have been cross-checked against USDA SR Legacy composites and retained as plausible estimates. 4. Primary cuisine is listed as Japanese-inspired in recognition of the Hokkaido milk component, though the beverage is sold and consumed in Singapore. 5. GI estimated at 55 (low — upper boundary of low GI range per University of Sydney classification: low ≤55, medium 56–69, high ≥70) based on analogous sweetened milk tea from University of Sydney GI Database; GL per 350 g serving = 21.0. gi_category corrected from 'medium' to 'low' as GI 55 falls at the upper boundary of the low range per the University of Sydney GI classification system. 6. trans_fat_g is 0.09 g/100 g, a trace amount consistent with naturally occurring trans fats in dairy (vaccenic acid); this is not an industrially added trans fat.

improvement_actions_taken: Iteration 1: Corrected ontology.foodon_match_type and ontology.foodex2_match_type from 'approximate' to 'closest_parent' per G5 feedback.

Iteration 2: Added mandatory local names for Chinese (simplified and traditional), Malay, Tamil, and Hindi to local_names and aliases_by_language to satisfy G6 multilingual naming compliance for Singaporean dishes. Ensured all new local names were added to the flat 'aliases' array.

Iteration 3: Corrected gi_category from 'medium' to 'low' — GI 55 falls at the upper boundary of the low GI range per the University of Sydney classification system (low ≤55, medium 56–69, high ≥70). Updated gi_source source_match_notes and notes field to document this correction.
